coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots for efficient delivery of curcumin in live cell
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Yadav, Himanshu
Rout, Debasish
Upadhyaya, Arun K.
Agarwala, Pratibha
Sharma, Akanksha
Sasmal, Dibyendu K.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) are a novel class of nanomaterials that has significant importance for applications in bioimaging, drug loading and delivery. Their easy preparation, tunable optoelectronic property, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, superior photostability, and exceptional water solubility contribute to their tremendous potentials for various applications. In this study, we synthesized carbon quantum dots using a greener and cost-efficient top-down reflux methods, and characterized the CQDs by various spectroscopy techniques. Furthermore, we investigated the potential application of CQDs as carrier of antitumor drug curcumin (Cur) in vitro by investigating the drug loading and release mechanism of the CQDs. Solubility of hydrophobic drug curcumin is increased when it forms complex with CQD, and hence increases the potential for its application. Moreover, the pH responsive drug release mechanism of the curcumin loaded CQDs is found to follow anomalous diffusion as per Korsmeyer-Peppas model. Additionally, we explored the in vivo bioimaging application of the CQDs, and used fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) technique using CQDs (as donor) and curcumin (as acceptor) to understand the heterogeneity of drug loading in live Escherichia coli (E. coli) cells. Our findings provide a detailed insight into the application of CQDs as probes in bioimaging and FRET, and as well as their potential for targeted curcumin delivery in live cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Drug delivery
Fret imaging
Live cell

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro pH dependent curcumin release kinetic study of CUR-CQDS
Application of CQDs and CUR-CQDS as probes for bioimaging and FRET study in live E. coli bacteria

We also like to thank IIT Jodhpur for SEED Grant. AS thanks CSIR for fellowship. PA and AKU thank to IITJ for fellowship. DR thank MoE for PMRF. DKS and all other authors thank department of chemistry and CASE for providing instrument facility.

DKS thank SERB, Department of Science and Technology for Start-up Research Grant (Grant number – SRG/2020/001730 ). We also like to thank IIT Jodhpur for SEED Grant. AS thanks CSIR for fellowship. PA and AKU thank to IITJ for fellowship. DR thank MoE for PMRF. DKS and all other authors thank department of chemistry and CASE for providing instrument facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Characterization of CQDs. (a) Excitation dependent emission spectra of CQDs at excitation wavelengths from 320 – 450 nm. Inset shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs with two shoulder absorption maxima
λ
m a x
= 265
n m
and
316 nm ,
(b) 3D Excitation Emission Matrix Spectrum (EEMS) of CQDs. (c) Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra of Calotropis gigantea (CG) extract and CQDs, (d) Raman Spectra of CQDs at 532 nm excitation wavelength, (e) Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) images of CQDs with its height profile revealing the size of the CQDs < 10 nm, (f) EDAX spectra of CQDs.
Quantum yield of CQDs. Area under the curve of fluorescence intensity vs absorbance plot for (a) C102, (b) CQDs.

Characterization of Cur-CQDs. (a) Excitation dependent emission spectra of Cur-CQDs at excitation wavelengths from 320–450 nm and inset shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs and Cur-CQDs, (b) 3D Excitation Emission Matrix Spectrum (EEMS) of Cur-CQDs, (c) Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra and (d) Raman Spectra (at 532 nm wavelength) of CQD, curcumin (Cur), and Cur-CQDs.

In vitro pH dependent curcumin release kinetics of Cur-CQDS. (a) Absorbance of Cur (in EtOH) at different concentrations, (b) Absorbance vs weight of curcumin Calibration curve, (c) In vitro drug release of curcumin from Cur-CQDs in PBS solution at pH 5.5 and pH 7.4 (37 °C), (d) Korsmeyer-Peppas model fit at pH 5.5 and pH 7.4.excitation-dependent emission in case of Cur-CQDs, when it was excited at different wavelengths. A red shift was also observed as discussed before in Section 3.1. Unlike in the case of CQDs, emission intensity of Cur-CQDs was observed to enhance at λem = 550 nm, when excited from 390 nm to 450 nm. It might be attributed to the presence of adsorbed curcumin over CQDs, which were not present in case of only CQDs. Bird's eye view of EEMS shown in Fig. 3b of Cur-CQDs observed to have an appearance of emission intensity in the region 530 - 560 nm due to the presence of curcumin in Cur-CQDs, further supporting curcumin loading over CQDs. The FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 3c observed to have two native curcumin peaks at 1275 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1 which were also present in case of Cur-CQDs. [63] The other -NH and -OH peaks were also conserved in the case of Cur-CQDs. Two native Raman peaks at 1600 cm−1 and 1626 cm−1 corresponding to the benzene ring of curcumin [64] (Fig. 3d) further confirmed the presence of curcumin over CQDs. Along with these two native peaks of curcumin, the peak at 1260 cm−1 (D band) and the peak at 1578 cm−1 (G band) were also present in case of Cur-CQDs.

FRET imaging of live cell using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and curcumin loaded carbon quantum dots (Cur-CQDs). (a1-a3) CQDs, (b1-b3) Cur-CQDs in E. coli (DH5α), (c) Spectral overlap of donor (CQDs) excitation and acceptor (curcumin) emission (d) An illustrative plot of heterogeneity for molecular level interaction in single E. coli bacterium using FRET efficiency. (e) FRET efficiency (CQDs and Curcumin) histogram calculated from 128 individual E. coli bacteria. The release mechanism is Fickian diffusion when the value of release exponent, n ≤ 0.5 and shows the non-Fickian diffusion when the value falls in the interval 0.5 < n < 1.0. [48] The value of n is found to be 0.53 and 0.54 at pH 5.5 and pH 7.4, respectively, which shows the release mechanism to be non-Fickian diffusion.

Top-down green aqueous synthetic route of CQDs using reflux method and curcumin loaded CQDs (Cur-CQDs). Leaves are used for green synthesis. CQDs fluorescence is shown in green. Then CQD-curcumin complex is prepared for efficient curcumin delivery. See Experimental Section for the detailed description of synthesis.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots for efficient delivery of curcumin in live cell

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Jodhpur, Rajasthan 342037, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Jodhpur, Rajasthan 342037, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author: Dibyendu K. Sasmal, Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Jodhpur, Web:

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) are a novel class of nanomaterials that has significant importance for applications in bioimaging, drug loading and delivery. Their easy preparation, tunable optoelectronic property, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, superior photostability, and exceptional water solubility contribute to their tremendous potentials for various applications. In this study, we synthesized carbon quantum dots using a greener and cost-efficient top-down reflux methods, and characterized the CQDs by various spectroscopy techniques. Furthermore, we investigated the potential application of CQDs as carrier of antitumor drug curcumin (Cur) in vitro by investigating the drug loading and release mechanism of the CQDs. Solubility of hydrophobic drug curcumin is increased when it forms complex with CQD, and hence increases the potential for its application. Moreover, the pH responsive drug release mechanism of the curcumin loaded CQDs is found to follow anomalous diffusion as per Korsmeyer-Peppas model. Additionally, we explored the in vivo bioimaging application of the CQDs, and used fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) technique using CQDs (as donor) and curcumin (as acceptor) to understand the heterogeneity of drug loading in live Escherichia coli (E. coli) cells. Our findings provide a detailed insight into the application of CQDs as probes in bioimaging and FRET, and as well as their potential for targeted curcumin delivery in live cells.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the accidental discovery of photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by Xu et al., in 2004, the emerging class of nanomaterials [1] have received tremendous attention, especially in nanomedicine [2] and biomedical applications [3] from the scientific community in last few years because of their promising potential for application in bioimaging [4], biosensing [5], drug loading thereby improving drug solubility [6], and targeted drug delivery [7] applications owing to their eminent biocompatibility [8], antioxidant and pro-oxidant nature [6], distinct optoelectronic properties [8], and easy to modify multiple surface functional groups [9]. Apart from these, their resistance to photobleaching [10], negligible phototoxicity [11], and tunable excitation-emission wavelength made them excellent candidate for bioimaging of live cells [12].There are various explanations for the origin of photoluminescence property of carbon quantum dots such as presence of various functional groups on its surface states, a π conjugated backbone, electron-hole combination and presence of fluorescent molecules that are connected to the surface or encapsulated inside carbon dots [13]. Further reports also suggests that CQDs do not possess the inherent property of photoluminescence, it is during its synthesis process where there is formation of molecular fluorophore which may contribute in the fluorescence of CQDs [14]. Recent observation of longer retention time of CQDs in tumor cells open up a new era to use them as photosensitizers to kill the tumor cells by photodynamic therapy [15]. Furthermore, these novel nanomaterials have also found non-biomedical applications in optical devices, printing ink, solar cells, and photocatalysis [8].

With the advancement of technology as yet numerous synthetic methods have been developed for the facile synthesis of CQDs with desired physicochemical and optical properties for various applications from a variety of precursors [16] including dual small molecule precursors, single small molecule precursors, natural polymer, biomass and synthetic polymers by using either top-down (laser ablation, arc discharge, and electrochemical oxidation) or bottom-up (microwave, thermal decomposition, hydrothermal, and solvothermal) approach [8,17]. In the current study, we have used a one-pot, cost efficient green synthesis reflux method to synthesize the CQDs from the leaves of Calotropis gigantea owing to not only its high carbon and nitrogen content but also its medicinal applications [18]. Calotropis gigantea also named as crown flower, is a plant native to Asian countries that shares many medicinal applications such as earache, epilepsy, sprain, anxiety and many more [19]. It also shows cytotoxic activity against several cancer, such as human colon cancer (WiDr [20], HCT-116 & HT-29 [21] cell lines) and human lung cancer (HeLa [22] cell line).

Over the years, curcumin (C21H20O6) also called diferuloylmethane, vital ingredient of turmeric [23] has shown promising application as antimicrobial [24], antiseptic [25], anti-inflammatory [26], and antioxidant drug [27]. In recent years, researchers explored the therapeutic effect of this ancient medical herb such as anti-angiogenic effect [28], suppression of proliferation of cancer cells [29], prevention of tumor invasion which implied its exceptional potential as an anti-cancer drug [30]. Despite the diversity and potential of curcumin as a multipurpose drug its less bioavailability [31] owing to its hydrophobicity [32], rapid degradation [33], poor absorption [34] still remains major challenge for its delivery and limit its effective pharmacodynamic efficacy inside the body. Along with the hydrophobicity, targeted delivery of curcumin in tumor cells is a critical step for treatment of cancer [35]. The non-polar nature of the drug also restricts the use of curcumin as a dye in bioimaging application as it couldn't enter the cytoplasm of a cell and bind to the outer lipid membrane [36].

In recent years, much needed light has been shed on simple, cost effective, and green preparation techniques for synthesizing carbon dots from natural, nontoxic precursors [1,3,8,36]. Also, the distinctive drug carrier properties of the CQDS due to manifold functional groups present on the surface have been explored [37]. pH dependent drug release mechanism of the CQDs made them potential drug carriers for targeted drug delivery in tumor cells as the tumor microenvironment is quite acidic as compared to the normal tissue [37,38]. Also, Miao et.al., Yuan et.al., reported improved solubility of hydrophobic drugs on loading to CQDs through π- π stacking interactions [39,40]. Although the CQDs have been found to be intrinsically capable of carrying drugs owing to multiple surface functional groups, it has yet to be explored whether their pH dependent drug release pattern follows Fickian diffusion or non-Fickian diffusion.

Here, we have synthesized CQDs from a natural precursor (leaves of Calotropis gigantea) using a simple reflux technique and characterized the CQDs by atomic force microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence emission spectroscopy, excitation-emission matrix spectroscopy (EEMS) and Fourier-transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. Subsequently, we have studied the application of the CQDs in bioimaging of live E. Coli bacteria. Furthermore, we have used CQDs and curcumin as donor and acceptor respectively to investigate heterogeneity in live Escherichia coli (E. coli) cell by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) technique. Additionally, we loaded curcumin on the CQDs to prepare curcumin loaded carbon quantum dots (Cur-CQDs) and studied the in vitro drug release mechanism of Cur-CQDs in response to pH through Korsmeyer and Peppas model. Collectively, these measurements shed new light on use of the novel biocompatible CQDs prepared from inexhaustible, natural, and inexpensive precursor not only as a probe for bioimaging but also as a carrier of intrinsically effective multipurpose natural drug curcumin. Additionally, Calotropis gigantea itself shows cytotoxicity, thus loading curcumin over CQDs (prepared from Calotropis gigantea) will be an overall efficient system for targeting the microenvironment of tumor cells.

Leaves of Calotropis gigantea were collected from the ground of IIT Jodhpur. Curcumin and Coumarin 102 (C102) were bought from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Luria broth (LB) was bought from Himedia, India and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) tablets (pH 7.4) were purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. (India). E. coli DH5α strain was received as a gift from Department of Bioscience and Bioengineering, IIT Jodhpur.

UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra were obtained by using LabIndia-2000 U UV/VIS Spectrophotometer and JASCO Spectrofluorometer FP-8300 respectively, using quartz cuvette of path length 1 cm. FTIR spectra were recorded using Bruker Alpha II FTIR spectrometer with Attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory. Raman Spectra were collected using Bayspec Raman Spectrophotometer with 532 nm laser. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) was done in order to find size using Park System XE 70. EDAX spectra was collected using SEM- EVO 18, EDS-51-ADDD-0048. All the bioimaging studies were done in Olympus IX83 inverted fluorescence microscope.

10 g of Calotropis gigantea leaves were taken and crushed using mortar and pestle. The paste obtained was diluted with 10 mL of MILLI-Q water in a 100 mL round bottom flask and refluxed for 2 h at 180 °C, which resulted in a brown residue. The residue was cooled to room temperature and filtered using Whatman filter paper. The filtrate was then centrifuged for 30 min at 14,500 rpm. The supernatant obtained was the CQD solution, which was stored at 4 °C in glass bottle wrapped with aluminum foil in dark environment.

0.5 mg curcumin was dissolved in 1 mL CQD solution and the mixture was stirred for three hours in dark. It was then centrifuged for 15 min at 1200 rpm to pelletize the unbound curcumin. The supernatant obtained was again centrifuged at 14,500 rpm for thirty minutes in order to pelletize the Cur-CQDs. The pellet was dried under vacuum overnight in order to remove moisture and stored in dark at 4 °C. The Cur-CQD pellet was dispersed in 500 µL milli-Q water, whenever needed.

The Fluorescence QY (Quantum yield) of CQDs was calculated using reference method using Coumarin 102 (C102) dye in water as reference [41]. Concentration of the reference solution of C102 and CQD solution was diluted with milli-Q water such that the UV–Vis absorption would have value < 0.1. The QY of CQDs was calculated using the following Equation [42]:
(1)
Φ
C Q D
=
Φ
s t d
.
G r a
d
C Q D
G r a
d
s t d
.
ɳ
C Q D
2
ɳ
s t d
2
where ΦCQD is the quantum yield of carbon quantum dot, Φstd is the quantum yield of C102 (0.764) [43,44], GradCQD, Gradstd is the gradient calculated from the integrated fluorescence intensity vs absorbance plot of CQDs and C102 respectively and ɳCQD and ɳstd is the refractive index (aqueous solution, ɳaq=1.33) of CQDs and C102 respectively.

The adsorption efficiency was calculated using the following Equation [45]:
(2)
A E
( % )
=
C T
−
C F
C T
×
100
where CT is the total curcumin added and CF is the free curcumin present after centrifugation at 1200 rpm.

To study the dependency of drug release based on pH change, 2 mL of Cur-CQDs were mixed in 50 mL of PBS solution at pH 5 and pH 7.4. It was continuously stirred in the dark at 80 rpm for 96 h in an incubator shaker at constant temperature of 37 °C. 2 mL of solution was taken out at pre-decided time intervals (0 to 96 h) and replaced with fresh PBS solution (2 mL). It was centrifuged at 1200 rpm to pelletize the released curcumin. The pellet was then dissolved in 2 mL EtOH and its absorbance was recorded. Using the calibration curve of Curcumin in EtOH (Fig. 4), weight was calculated for each pellet to find the percentage of drug release for each time interval. Release percentage [46] was calculated using the below Equation:
(3)
%
R e l e a s e =
C R
C T
×
100
in which CT = total curcumin loaded over the CQDs and CR = released curcumin.

The Korsmeyer and Peppas Model [47,48] has been used to analyze the release of curcumin from Cur-CQD using the following Equation:
(4)
f
( t )
=
M t
M ∞
= k
t
n
where f(t) is the fraction of drug release at a particular time t, Mt is the amount of drug release at time t, Mꝏ is the amount of drug release at infinite time (i.e., the total drug release), n is associated with the different release mechanisms and k is the rate constant.

E. coli (E. coli, DH5α) bacteria were cultured in laboratory using an incubator shaker. 10 mL Luria Broth (LB) solution was taken in a 15 mL centrifuge tube followed by addition of 0.2 μL E. coli strain and was incubated in an incubator shaker at 120 rpm for 15 h at 37 °C. The bacterial solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min followed by washing with PBS solution (pH = 7.4) thrice to remove the excess media from the bacterial solution.

For incubation of CQDs (or Cur-CQDs/C102/Curcumin) in the bacteria, bacterial pellet was dispersed in 1 mL Milli-Q water. The optimal concentration of sample was added to this solution and placed in an incubator shaker at 120 rpm for 3 h at 37 °C. The solution was centrifuged and washed thrice with PBS solution (and 5% EtOH in water in case of curcumin samples) to remove the unincubated CQDs from the solution. Finally, the bacterial pellet was dispersed in 300 μL PBS at pH 7.4.

2 μL of the dispersed bacterial solution was put on a 70 × 25 mm glass slide, covered using a 0.17 mm cover glass of size 22 × 22 mm. For immobilization, the ends of the cover glass were sealed using nail-polish. Bioimaging was done using the Olympus IX83 inverted fluorescence microscope at different excitation and emission wavelengths. There were three sets of excitation and emission wavelengths in order to avoid the overlap of emission intensity of incubated sample: (excitation/emission, nm) 345/455 (Blue channel), 494/518 (Green channel), 555/580 (Red Channel). At 345/455 and 494/518 CQDs and Cur-CQDs were fluorescent in all three channels in bacteria.

For the FRET study in bacteria, CQD (Donor), Curcumin (acceptor) and CQD and Curcumin (Donor + Acceptor) were incubated in E. coli. For bioimaging, there were two separate sets of excitation and emission wavelengths to avoid the overlap of fluorescence emission: 320/385 (Blue channel), 320/530 (Red Channel). The images were collected at these two sets of wavelengths for the above three samples (donor, acceptor, and donor + acceptor). The other parameters, such as exposure time, intensity of light, etc. were kept same for all sets of data. The FRET efficiency was calculated by using the Equation: [49]
(5)
E =
I
A
( D )
I
A
( D )
+
I D
where, E is the FRET efficiency, IA(D) is the intensity of acceptor in presence of donor and ID is the intensity of only donor.

The FT-IR spectra was recorded to analyze the presence of various functional groups in CQDs (Fig. 1c). The peak at 1740 cm−1 corresponds to the carbonyl group present in the carbon quantum dots. [53] The peak at 1650 cm−1 is due to the C = C, and C = O (of amide) bond, 1550 cm−1 is due to the NO stretching, 1385 cm−1 corresponds to the OH stretching and at 1090 cm−1 is due to the presence of CO group in the CQDs. [54] The peak in the region of 2800–3000 cm−1 is due to the CH stretching and the IR stretching in 3200 cm−1 to 3600 cm−1 corresponds to the OH and NH bonds. [55] The presence of -OH and -NH groups increase the solubility of CQDs in water, making them hydrophilic in nature [56]. It is to be found that the above peaks are also present in case of Calotropis gigantea (CG) leaves extract. Additionally the peaks at 2487 cm−1 and 2407 cm−1 in CG extract are present because of the conjugated C = C bonds and CC bonds which are disturbed during the formation of carbon quantum dots [57,58]. The Raman spectra of CQDs was collected using 532 nm laser, the peak at 1260 cm−1 corresponds to the D band of the sp3 carbon atoms and the peak at 1578 cm−1 corresponds to G band of the sp2 carbon atoms. [59] The D band is due to the out of plane vibrations of sp3 hybridised carbon atoms and G band corresponds to the in-plane vibrations of sp2 hybridised carbon atoms of CQDs. [60] The AFM image shown in Fig. 1e and its height profile verified that the size CQDs falling in the quantum region (< 10 nm). The EDAX spectra was collected using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to detect the various elements which were present in the CQDs. The EDAX spectrum (Fig. 1f) shows the presence of various elements such as O, C, S, P.

The quantum yield (QY) was calculated using Coumarin 102 (QY = 0.764) [43] as a reference and the QY of CQDs was found to be 0.58 using Eq. (1) (Fig. 2 ), which is excellent for quantum dots derived from natural precursor.

Curcumin is an anticancer agent as it inhibits the cell cycle by inhibiting the S phase of the cell cycle. [61,62] But it has its limited application as an anticancer agent due to its hydrophobic nature, rapid degradation, low bioavailability and poor absorption [31–33]. CQDs can also be used as a surface to adsorb curcumin due to the various functional groups present on CQDs’ surface. The synthesis of curcumin loaded carbon quantum dots (Cur-CQDs) was discussed in materials and methods section (Scheme 1). The curcumin adsorption efficiency on the surface of the CQDs was calculated using Eq. (2) and found to be 29%. Curcumin binding to carbon dots (curcumin loading) might be attributed to the presence of electrostatic interaction between the various functional groups on the CQDs and curcumin.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra shown in inset of 3a with a peak at 320 nm owing to n→π* transition was also found to present in the Cur-CQDs. The absorption peak (λmax) at 427 nm for curcumin was also present in case of Cur-CQDs supporting curcumin loading to the CQDs. Generally, Curcumin shows low solubility in water due to its hydrophobic nature but the Cur-CQDs found to form homogeneous solution in water. The fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 3 a) also showed

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro pH dependent curcumin release kinetic study of CUR-CQDS
ce:section.ce:para: At various concentrations of curcumin in ethanol (Fig. 4 a) absorbance was recorded. Further, absorbance vs weight calibration curve (Fig. 4b) was plotted. The percentage drug release from Cur-CQDs was calculated using Eq. (4). The curcumin release pattern was studied at two pH conditions, at pH 7.4 and pH 5.5 (Fig. 4c). pH 7.4 was chosen as it is physiological condition, whereas pH 5.5 is the pH of microenvironment of tumor cells [65]. At the initial phase, the maximum curcumin burst was observed to be 28% and 17% at pH 5.5 and pH 7.4 in 2 h, respectively. In case of pH 5.5 the percentage of drug release was found to be more i.e., 73% and only 44% drug was released at pH 7.4 at the end of 96 h This might be because of the weaker interaction between curcumin and CQDs in acidic conditions, whereas stronger interaction at physiological pH (7.4). The above observation is critical for potential use of Cur-CQDs in targeted curcumin delivery in tumor cells as maximum release of curcumin was observed at acidic pH.

The percentage of drug release data was fitted using Korsmeyer and Peppas model [47] (Fig. 4d) to analyze the kinetics of curcumin release from Cur-CQDs (using Eq. (4)) i.e.,
f
( t )
= k
t
n
, where f(t) is the fraction of drug released and n is the release exponent and k is the rate release constant). The value of ‘n’ gives information about the drug release

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs and CUR-CQDS as probes for bioimaging and FRET study in live E. coli bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli is a highly versatile and ubiquitous gastrointestinal symbiotic bacterium in human and other mammals. [66] These are rod shaped gram-negative bacteria with outer lipopolysaccharide membrane and inner cytoplasmic membrane with a periplasmic space in between the two layers. [67] It is a widely used model bacteria for biotechnology, bioengineering, and live cell imaging owing to their simple facile culture, easy genomic manipulation and prolonged lab history. [66,68] CQDs and Cur-CQDs were incubated inside live E. coli bacteria as discussed in experimental section. There were three sets of excitation and emission wavelengths for bioimaging, 345/455, 494/518, and 555/580. From the emission spectra, CQDs were found to show fluoresce signals in 345/455 and 494/518 channel (Fig. 5 a1 and 5a2) but in 555/580 channel (Fig. 5a3) no fluorescence was observed. Fig. 5a3 could be considered as a control image in case of only CQDs. The Cur-CQDs showed fluorescence in all the three channels (Fig. 5b1-5b3). The 555/580 channel was set only to see the fluorescence of curcumin (Fig. 5b3) as CQDs wouldn't fluoresce in this channel (Fig. 5a3). Appearance of fluorescence image in 555/580 channel supported the fact that there is adsorption of curcumin on the CQDs in Cur-CQDs. The appearance of fluorescent images in all the three channels (Fig. 5b) props up the fact that penetration of Cur-CQDs were also successful in live E. coli bacteria.

FRET (Fluorescence resonance energy transfer) also known as spectroscopic ruler, a highly reliable and sensitive spectroscopic technique was employed to study the heterogeneity at molecular level by the interactions between CQDs and curcumin in live E. coli cells. A significant spectral overlap between emission of CQDs (donor) and absorption of curcumin (acceptor) was shown in Fig. 5c. The property of excitation-dependent tunable emission spectra of CQDs was used to study the FRET with curcumin in live cell. FRET imaging was done to calculate the FRET efficiency using Eq. (5). Fig. 5d shows the heterogeneity in FRET efficiency in a single live E. coli bacterium, which shows a heterogeneity in FRET efficiency ranging from 0.53 to 0.71 that shows the molecular level interaction of CQD and curcumin in live E. coli bacterium. Fig. 5e shows the histogram of FRET efficiency of 128 individual E. coli bacteria which shows the heterogeneity in FRET efficiency, with a low FRET value of 0.53 and high FRET value of 0.69. Low FRET could be because of the donor-acceptor in the peptidoglycan wall in between the double membrane of E. coli and the high FRET could be because of the strong interaction of CQDs and curcumin inside E. coli.

In final discussion, CQDs derived from the natural precursor show outstanding biocompatibility, excellent water solubility and exceptional probes for bioimaging. Curcumin delivery inside the live cell by the Cur-CQDs as shown in Fig. 5b3 confirms the increased solubility of the hydrophobic drug and thereby increasing its bioavailability, which has been a major challenge with anti-tumor drug curcumin. In vitro drug release kinetics analysed by Korsmeyer-Peppas model (Fig. 4c and 4d) showing anomalous diffusion and better drug release at acidic pH 5.5 makes Cur-CQDs promising probes for targeted curcumin delivery in acidic environment such as tumor cells. Curcumin being an anticancer drug, thus can be specifically delivered at tumor sites for its efficient pharmacodynamic action on tumor cells. The low cost and simple preparation technique from easily available natural precursors make the CQDs economical nanodrug carriers and their potential could be explored for the use of CQDs in delivery of other important hydrophobic drugs, which will improve their efficacy and specificity.

In summary, we have developed a one-step facile and robust approach for green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQD), and applied as efficient drug delivery system in live cell. Specifically, the CQDs have been successfully utilized as probes for bioimaging and FRET donor in E. coli. Additionally, we have enhanced the delivery of curcumin by using the CQDs that has excellent property as a drug (small molecule) carrier. Both CQDs and Cur-CQDs have exhibited excellent biocompatibility and promising potential as probes for intracellular bioimaging and FRET imaging. Heterogeneity in FRET efficiency has been observed in cell by using CQDs and curcumin as donor and acceptor respectively, which clearly suggests varied polarity and heterogeneity in drug delivery in live cell. In vitro studies provide important insights for the use of Cur-CQDs to improve the curcumin bioavailability. The CQDs demonstrate strong binding at physiological pH (7.4) and weak binding at acidic pH (5.5), following anomalous diffusion kinetics, making them potential probes for pH dependent curcumin delivery.
DKS supervised the project. HY performed the experiment. All authors contributed in manuscript writing.
Data: Raw data will be available after reasonable request to corresponding author.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: DKS thank SERB, Department of Science and Technology for Start-up Research Grant (Grant number – SRG/2020/001730). We also like to thank IIT Jodhpur for SEED Grant. AS thanks CSIR for fellowship. PA and AKU thank to IITJ for fellowship. DR thank MoE for PMRF. DKS and all other authors thank department of chemistry and CASE for providing instrument facility.
